info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

questiontext.text: The characteristic polynomial of \(A = \begin{pmatrix}3 & 1 \\ 0 & 3\end{pmatrix}\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det(A-\lambda I)=\det\begin{pmatrix}3-\lambda&1\\0&3-\lambda\end{pmatrix}=(3-\lambda)^2\).
Incorrect. \(\text{tr}(A)=6\), \(\det(A)=9\), so the char. poly. is \(\lambda^2-6\lambda+9=(\lambda-3)^2\).
Incorrect. The only eigenvalue is 3 (repeated); the characteristic polynomial is \((\lambda-3)^2\).
Incorrect. \(\det(A-\lambda I) = (3-\lambda)^2 = \lambda^2-6\lambda+9\neq\lambda^2-9\).

questiontext.text: A real symmetric matrix must have:

answer.text: All eigenvalues real and an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors (spectral theorem)

feedback.text: Correct. The spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices guarantees real eigenvalues and an orthogonal eigenbasis.
Incorrect. Symmetric matrices can have negative or zero eigenvalues (e.g., \(\begin{pmatrix}-1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\)).
Incorrect. A symmetric matrix can have distinct eigenvalues (e.g., the identity has all eigenvalues equal, but diagonal matrices with distinct entries are symmetric).
Incorrect. Symmetric matrices can have repeated eigenvalues (e.g., the identity matrix \(I_n\) has eigenvalue 1 with multiplicity \(n\)).

questiontext.text: Let \(A = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4\end{pmatrix}\). The characteristic polynomial is \(p(\lambda)=\lambda^2-5\lambda-2\). By Cayley-Hamilton, \(A^2\) equals:

feedback.text: Correct. Cayley-Hamilton: \(A^2 - 5A - 2I = 0\), so \(A^2 = 5A + 2I\). Verify: \(A^2=\begin{pmatrix}7&10\\15&22\end{pmatrix}\) and \(5A+2I=\begin{pmatrix}7&10\\15&22\end{pmatrix}\). ✓
Incorrect. The characteristic polynomial is \(\lambda^2-5\lambda-2\), so \(A^2=5A+2I\), not \(5A-2I\).
Incorrect. By Cayley-Hamilton: \(A^2 = 5A + 2I\).
Incorrect. The correct relation is \(A^2 = 5A + 2I\) from \(p(A)=0\).

questiontext.text: For a linear operator \(T\) on a finite-dimensional space, the geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue \(\lambda\) is \(\dim\ker(T-\lambda I)\) and algebraic multiplicity is its multiplicity as a root of the characteristic polynomial. Which always holds?

feedback.text: Correct. The geometric multiplicity never exceeds the algebraic multiplicity. Equality for all eigenvalues is equivalent to diagonalizability.
Incorrect. For \(A=\begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&3\end{pmatrix}\), eigenvalue 3 has algebraic multiplicity 2 but geometric multiplicity 1.
Incorrect. This is backwards; the geometric multiplicity is bounded above by the algebraic multiplicity.
Incorrect. Eigenvalues can have algebraic multiplicity greater than 1 (repeated roots of the characteristic polynomial).

questiontext.text: If \(A\) is an \(n\times n\) matrix with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\) (counted with multiplicity), then:

feedback.text: Correct. These follow from the factored form of the characteristic polynomial: \(p(\lambda) = \prod(\lambda_i - \lambda)\).
Incorrect. Trace equals the sum of eigenvalues; determinant equals the product. These are swapped here.
Incorrect. Trace is the sum of all eigenvalues, not \(n\) times one eigenvalue.
Incorrect. This is false in general; e.g., \(A=I_2\) gives \(\det=1\) and \(\text{tr}^2=4\).

name.text: LA11-Q06 Power of Matrix via Eigendecomposition

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) be diagonalizable with \(A = PDP^{-1}\) where \(D = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n)\). Then \(A^k\) for positive integer \(k\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(A^k = (PDP^{-1})^k = PD^kP^{-1}\) since intermediate \(P^{-1}P = I\) terms collapse.
Incorrect. \(A^k = PD^kP^{-1}\); the eigenvector matrix \(P\) appears exactly once on each side.
Incorrect. \(A^k = PD^kP^{-1}\), not \(k\) times \(A\).
Incorrect. The \(k\)-th power of a diagonal matrix \(D\) raises each diagonal entry to the \(k\)-th power, not shifts by \(k\).

questiontext.text: The Gershgorin circle theorem states: every eigenvalue of \(A = (a_{ij})\) lies in at least one Gershgorin disk \(D_i = \{\lambda : |\lambda - a_{ii}| \leq \sum_{j\neq i}|a_{ij}|\}\). For \(A = \begin{pmatrix}4 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 5\end{pmatrix}\), all eigenvalues lie in the union of disks centered at:

feedback.text: Incorrect. The centers are the diagonal entries (4, 3, 5) and the radii are the row sums of off-diagonal absolute values.
Incorrect. The radii are the off-diagonal row sums, not the diagonal entries themselves.
Incorrect. The Gershgorin disks are centered at the diagonal entries, not at 0.

questiontext.text: If \(Q\) is an orthogonal matrix (\(Q^TQ = I\)), then all eigenvalues of \(Q\) have:

answer.text: Modulus equal to 1 (lie on the unit circle in \(\mathbb{C}\))

feedback.text: Correct. If \(Qv=\lambda v\), then \(\|Qv\|=\|v\|\) (orthogonal maps preserve norm), so \(|\lambda|\|v\|=\|v\|\), giving \(|\lambda|=1\).
Incorrect. Real eigenvalues of orthogonal matrices are \(\pm1\); complex eigenvalues occur in conjugate pairs on the unit circle.
Incorrect. Orthogonal matrices can have eigenvalue \(-1\) (e.g., reflections) as well as complex eigenvalues of modulus 1.
Incorrect. The trace (sum of eigenvalues) of an orthogonal matrix is the sum of diagonal entries, which can be any value in \([-n,n]\).

questiontext.text: Let \(P\) be an \(n\times n\) matrix with \(P^2 = P\) (idempotent/projection). The eigenvalues of \(P\) are:

feedback.text: Correct. If \(Pv=\lambda v\), then \(P^2v=\lambda^2v=Pv=\lambda v\), so \(\lambda^2=\lambda\), giving \(\lambda=0\) or \(\lambda=1\).
Incorrect. For a non-zero projection, 1 is always an eigenvalue (any vector in the image of \(P\)).
Incorrect. For a projection that is not the identity, 0 is always an eigenvalue (any vector in the kernel of \(P\)).
Incorrect. The equation \(\lambda^2=\lambda\) restricts eigenvalues to 0 and 1.

questiontext.text: The Perron-Frobenius theorem states that a matrix with all positive entries has a unique largest real eigenvalue (the Perron root) with a strictly positive eigenvector. For \(A = \begin{pmatrix}2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2\end{pmatrix}\), the Perron root is:

feedback.text: Correct. Eigenvalues: \(\det\begin{pmatrix}2-\lambda&1\\1&2-\lambda\end{pmatrix}=(\lambda-1)(\lambda-3)=0\). So \(\lambda=3\) (Perron root) with eigenvector \((1,1)^T\) and \(\lambda=1\) with eigenvector \((1,-1)^T\).
Incorrect. The Perron root is the largest eigenvalue, which is 3, not 1. The eigenvector \((1,-1)^T\) has a negative entry, not "strictly positive".
Incorrect. The characteristic polynomial gives eigenvalues 1 and 3, not 4.
Incorrect. 2 is not an eigenvalue of this matrix.

questiontext.text: Let \(A\) and \(B = P^{-1}AP\) be similar matrices. Which statement is FALSE?

feedback.text: Correct (this is FALSE). If \(Av=\lambda v\), then \(B(P^{-1}v)=\lambda(P^{-1}v)\), so the eigenvectors are related by \(P^{-1}\) but are generally different.
Incorrect (this is TRUE). Similar matrices share the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues.
Incorrect (this is TRUE). \(\det(B)=\det(P^{-1}AP)=\det(P^{-1})\det(A)\det(P)=\det(A)\).
Incorrect (this is TRUE). \(\text{tr}(ABC)=\text{tr}(BCA)\), so \(\text{tr}(P^{-1}AP)=\text{tr}(APP^{-1})=\text{tr}(A)\).

questiontext.text: Two diagonalizable matrices \(A\) and \(B\) can be simultaneously diagonalized (by the same matrix \(P\)) if and only if:

feedback.text: Correct. Two diagonalizable matrices can be simultaneously diagonalized iff they commute. This follows from the fact that commuting diagonalizable matrices share an eigenbasis.
Incorrect. Equal determinants do not imply commutativity or simultaneous diagonalizability.
Incorrect. Equal traces do not suffice for simultaneous diagonalizability.
Incorrect. Equal rank is not the criterion; commutativity is required.

name.text: LA11-Q13 Min Polynomial Divides Char Polynomial

questiontext.text: For an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\), let \(m(\lambda)\) be the minimal polynomial and \(p(\lambda)\) the characteristic polynomial. Which of the following is always true?

answer.text: \(m(\lambda)\) divides \(p(\lambda)\) and they have the same roots (over the algebraic closure)

feedback.text: Correct. By Cayley-Hamilton, \(p(A)=0\) so \(m\mid p\). By Cayley-Hamilton and the definition of minimal polynomial, both polynomials have the same set of roots.
Incorrect. For \(A=I_n\), \(m(\lambda)=\lambda-1\) but \(p(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)^n\). These differ when \(n>1\).
Incorrect. The minimal polynomial divides the characteristic polynomial, not the other way around.
Incorrect. \(\deg m = n\) iff \(A\) has a cyclic vector (e.g., companion matrix). In general, \(\deg m \leq n\).

questiontext.text: A real symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite iff \(x^TAx > 0\) for all \(x\neq 0\). Equivalently, \(A\) is positive definite iff:

answer.text: All eigenvalues of \(A\) are strictly positive

feedback.text: Correct. By the spectral theorem, a real symmetric \(A\) is positive definite iff all its eigenvalues are positive.
Incorrect. \(\det(A)>0\) is necessary but not sufficient (e.g., \(A=\begin{pmatrix}-1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\) has \(\det=1>0\) but is negative definite).

answer.text: All diagonal entries of \(A\) are positive

feedback.text: Incorrect. Positive diagonal entries don't guarantee positive definiteness (off-diagonal terms matter).
Incorrect. Positive trace (sum of eigenvalues) doesn't mean all eigenvalues are positive.

questiontext.text: For a real symmetric matrix \(A\) with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1 \leq \lambda_2 \leq \cdots \leq \lambda_n\), the Rayleigh quotient \(R(x) = \frac{x^TAx}{x^Tx}\) satisfies:

answer.text: \(\lambda_1 \leq R(x) \leq \lambda_n\) for all \(x\neq 0\), with equality achieved

feedback.text: Correct. The Rayleigh quotient is bounded by the smallest and largest eigenvalues, achieved at the corresponding eigenvectors.
Incorrect. The Rayleigh quotient varies with \(x\); it equals the average eigenvalue only for specific choices of \(x\).
Incorrect. The maximum of the Rayleigh quotient is the largest eigenvalue \(\lambda_n\), not the trace.
Incorrect. The Rayleigh quotient for a unit vector is \(x^TAx\), which ranges between \(\lambda_1\) and \(\lambda_n\).

name.text: LA11-Q16 Eigenvalues of Block Triangular Matrix

questiontext.text: Let \(A = \begin{pmatrix}B & C \\ 0 & D\end{pmatrix}\) be a block upper triangular matrix where \(B\) is \(p\times p\) and \(D\) is \(q\times q\). The eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

answer.text: The union of eigenvalues of \(B\) and eigenvalues of \(D\)

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det(A-\lambda I) = \det(B-\lambda I)\det(D-\lambda I)\), so the characteristic polynomial factors into those of \(B\) and \(D\).

answer.text: The product of eigenvalues of \(B\) and eigenvalues of \(D\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. The eigenvalues are the union (combined set), not products of individual eigenvalues.

answer.text: Only the eigenvalues of \(B\) (block \(D\) is ignored)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Both diagonal blocks contribute their eigenvalues to the spectrum of \(A\).
Incorrect. The off-diagonal block \(C\) does not contribute eigenvalues; only the diagonal blocks \(B\) and \(D\) do.

questiontext.text: A stochastic matrix (Markov matrix) has all entries non-negative and all column sums equal to 1. Which statement is always true for a stochastic matrix?

answer.text: \(\lambda = 1\) is always an eigenvalue, and all eigenvalues satisfy \(|\lambda| \leq 1\)

feedback.text: Correct. Column sums = 1 means \((1,1,\ldots,1)^T\) is a left eigenvector with eigenvalue 1. By Gershgorin (or direct argument), all eigenvalues have modulus \(\leq 1\).
Incorrect. Stochastic matrices can have other eigenvalues (e.g., 0, -1, complex numbers on the unit disk).
Incorrect. An invertible stochastic matrix (e.g., the identity) has no zero eigenvalue.

answer.text: All eigenvalues are positive real numbers

feedback.text: Incorrect. Stochastic matrices can have complex eigenvalues or negative real eigenvalues (e.g., permutation matrices).

questiontext.text: The singular values of a matrix \(A\) are the non-negative square roots of eigenvalues of \(A^TA\). For a real symmetric positive definite matrix \(A\), the singular values are:

feedback.text: Correct. For symmetric positive definite \(A\), \(A^TA = A^2\) has eigenvalues \(\lambda_i^2\), so singular values are \(\sqrt{\lambda_i^2} = \lambda_i > 0\) (eigenvalues of \(A\)).

answer.text: Equal to the square of the eigenvalues of \(A\)

feedback.text: Incorrect. Singular values equal eigenvalues (for symmetric PD), not their squares.
Incorrect. For symmetric positive definite matrices, singular values equal eigenvalues.
Incorrect. Singular values of a positive definite matrix equal its positive eigenvalues, which are generally not all 1.

questiontext.text: The companion matrix of a monic polynomial \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^n + a_{n-1}\lambda^{n-1}+\cdots+a_0\) is the \(n\times n\) matrix \(C\) whose characteristic polynomial is \(p(\lambda)\). If \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^3 - 6\lambda^2 + 11\lambda - 6 = (\lambda-1)(\lambda-2)(\lambda-3)\), the eigenvalues of \(C\) are:

feedback.text: Correct. The characteristic polynomial of the companion matrix is exactly \(p(\lambda)\), so its eigenvalues are the roots of \(p\): \(\lambda = 1, 2, 3\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalues are the roots of \(p(\lambda)\), which are 1, 2, 3 (all positive).
Incorrect. These are the (negatives of) coefficients of \(p\), not its roots.
Incorrect. The roots of \(\lambda^3-6\lambda^2+11\lambda-6\) are 1, 2, and 3.

questiontext.text: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of an invertible matrix \(A\), then the corresponding eigenvalue of \(A^{-1}\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. If \(Av=\lambda v\) and \(\lambda\neq0\), then \(v=A^{-1}(Av)=\lambda A^{-1}v\), so \(A^{-1}v=(1/\lambda)v\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalue of \(A^{-1}\) is \(1/\lambda\), not \(-\lambda\).
Incorrect. \(\lambda^2\) is the eigenvalue of \(A^2\), not \(A^{-1}\).
Incorrect. The eigenvalue of \(A^{-1}\) is \(1/\lambda\), and the eigenvector is the same.
